column1: ai agent for game,"Below is a short‑list of the most relevant open‑source projects from the GitHub results you posted, together with a quick “why you might pick it” summary.  I’ve also added a couple of widely‑used complementary toolkits that often show up in game‑AI pipelines, in case you need something that isn’t covered by the list.

column4: A **multi‑agent language‑game environment** built on top of the Farama Foundation’s Gymnasium API. It lets you spin up dozens of LLM‑based agents that can chat, cooperate, or compete in a sandbox “arena”.
column5:
• Prototyping dialogue‑heavy NPCs or AI teammates that need natural‑language interaction.
• Research on emergent communication, negotiation, or coordination among LLMs.
column6:
• Uses the familiar Gymnasium interface, so you can plug in any RL or prompting library (e.g., RL‑HF, LangChain).
• Comes with ready‑made game‑like tasks (capture‑the‑flag, resource‑gathering) that you can extend.
• Actively maintained by the Farama Foundation, which also backs the popular Gymnasium ecosystem.

column4: Code for **Cicero**, an AI that plays the board game *Diplomacy* using open‑domain natural‑language negotiation. Includes the dialogue model, a policy network, and a game engine.
column5:
• Building AI that must **negotiate, form alliances, or bluff** in a multiplayer setting.
• Studying large‑scale language‑grounded decision making.
column6:
• Demonstrates a full pipeline: from language model → policy → game actions.
• Provides a concrete example of how to combine LLM prompting with a strategic planner, which you can adapt to any turn‑based strategy game.

column4: A **Python‑side RL wrapper for the Godot game engine**. It uses the Godot‑Python bridge to expose the game state to OpenAI‑Gym‑style agents and supports PPO, DQN, etc., via Stable‑Baselines3.
column5:
• When your game is built in **Godot** and you want to train NPCs with reinforcement learning.
• Rapid prototyping of learning‑based behaviors (e.g., enemy chasing, platforming, puzzle solving).
column6:
• No need to write custom networking code – the bridge handles state sync.
• Works with any Gym‑compatible RL library, letting you swap algorithms quickly.
• Open‑source and fairly lightweight, making it easy to embed in CI pipelines.

column4: A **C++ library of classic steering behaviors** (seek, flee, flocking, path following, etc.) for autonomous characters.
column5:
• Classic “boids”‑style crowd simulation, vehicle AI, or any game that needs **real‑time, deterministic movement** without heavy ML.
• Projects where you prefer a compiled‑language solution for performance.
column6:
• Very mature (originally from 2000s) and battle‑tested in many commercial titles.
• Header‑only, no external dependencies, easy to drop into any C++ engine (Unreal, custom).
• Good learning resource for understanding the math behind steering.

column4: A **Minecraft‑specific AI framework** that wraps the game’s server API and provides tools for training agents (RL, imitation, scripted).
column5:
• If you are building **Minecraft bots, automated builders, or research agents** that interact with the block world.
• Teaching RL concepts in a sandbox environment.
column6:
• Handles the tedious part of connecting to a live Minecraft server, managing chunks, inventories, and actions.
• Includes example agents and a simple training loop, so you can get a prototype running in minutes.

column1: ## How to choose the right tool

column4: 1️⃣ Define a game‑state → 2️⃣ Prompt an LLM (OpenAI, Anthropic, Llama‑2, etc.) → 3️⃣ Parse the response into an in‑game action.  Both repos give you a ready‑made loop you can replace the underlying model with.
1️⃣ Export game state as a Gym observation → 2️⃣ Train with Stable‑Baselines3 / RLlib → 3️⃣ Deploy the learned policy back into the engine via the bridge.
1️⃣ Use the provided policy network as a template → 2️⃣ Swap in your own game rules → 3️⃣ Fine‑tune on domain‑specific dialogue data.
1️⃣ Add steering behavior objects to your character class → 2️⃣ Combine them (e.g., flock + obstacle avoidance) → 3️⃣ Tune weights for the desired feel.
1️⃣ Spin up a local Minecraft server via the provided Dockerfile → 2️⃣ Write an agent that observes block data → 3️⃣ Train with RL or scripted policies.

column1: ## Complementary toolkits you might also want to keep in your toolbox

column4: Official Unity bridge for RL, imitation learning, and behavior cloning. Works with any Unity project, and includes a visual editor for curriculum learning.
Scalable RL library that can run thousands of parallel environments (useful if you want to train many agents in `chatarena` or `godot_rl_agents`).
Prompt‑engineering framework for chaining LLM calls, useful when you need multi‑step reasoning (e.g., “plan → ask for resources → execute in game”).
Standard interface that both `chatarena` and `godot_rl_agents` already expose; most RL libraries expect this API.
Containerizing your game server + agent training environment makes experiments reproducible and isolates heavy GPU workloads.

column1: # 3️⃣ Run the example Godot project (requires Godot 4.x)
The same pattern works for `chatarena` (replace the Gym env name with `ChatArena-v0`) or for `MineStudio` (the `MineEnv` class is also Gym‑compatible).
* **For LLM‑driven dialogue/negotiation:** start with **`chatarena`** (general multi‑agent) or **`diplomacy_cicero`** (turn‑based negotiation).
* **For RL in a game engine you control:** **`godot_rl_agents`** (Godot) or **Unity ML‑Agents** (Unity) give you a ready bridge to stable‑baselines/RLlib.
* **For classic movement without ML:** **`OpenSteer`** is lightweight and battle‑tested.
